title: Doughnut holes
subtitle: Small fried yeast dough balls rolled in cinnamon sugar
excerpt: Small, round fried dough balls made from standard yeast doughnut dough and rolled in cinnamon sugar while still warm. The scaled-down format means they cook through quickly in the hot oil and are easier to portion and serve than full-sized doughnuts. Eaten in one or two bites, they have a tender, pillowy interior and a lightly crisp, sugar-dusted exterior.
sourceNotes: Small fried dough balls are documented in American and European baking literature from the nineteenth century onward. Fannie Farmer, The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (first edition 1896, fully in the public domain and available at Project Gutenberg), describes fried doughnut dough in ball form. The term 'doughnut holes' as the small, round centre pieces of ring doughnuts (or separately fried small rounds) is documented in American domestic cookery guides from the early twentieth century.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten immediately. The proved, uncooked balls can be refrigerated for up to 8 hours; bring to room temperature for 20 minutes before frying.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Deep-fried in vegetable oil at 175°C. Small balls cook in 1 to 2 minutes per side; watch carefully as they colour quickly.

glossaryTerms.definition: Rolling a warm fried doughnut hole in a mixture of caster sugar and ground cinnamon immediately after it comes out of the oil, while the surface is still slightly tacky. The small amount of oil on the surface acts as the adhesive that makes the sugar cling. If the doughnuts cool completely before rolling, the sugar will not stick. Mix about 1 teaspoon of cinnamon per 100 g of caster sugar for a balance that is distinct without overpowering.

content.text: Doughnut holes are exactly what they sound like: small portions of doughnut dough, proved, and fried until golden. The
cinnamon sugar must go on while the dough is still warm
from the oil so it adheres. Have the cinnamon sugar in a shallow bowl ready next to the fryer. Fry in small batches and roll each batch as it comes out.

items.prepNote: mixed with the 150 g caster sugar for rolling

content.text: Mix the flour, yeast, salt, and sugar. Add the warm milk, egg, vanilla, and soft butter and mix to a smooth dough. Knead for 8 minutes. Cover and leave for 60 minutes until doubled. Divide into portions of about 20 g each (golf-ball-sized) and roll into smooth balls. Place on a lined tray, cover, and prove for 30 minutes.

Heat the oil to 175°C. Fry the dough balls in batches of 6 to 8 for 1 to 2 minutes per side until evenly deep golden. Remove with a slotted spoon and toss immediately in the cinnamon sugar. Serve warm.
